caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than 326122.
a: Task exposure is inferred rather than measured.
a: The estimate may miss differences between continuous pipe extrusion and more labor-intensive fitting fabrication or molding.
rho: No five-year implementation study specific to plastic pipe was found.
rho: Adoption may be faster at standardized, high-volume plants and slower for short-run fittings or fabricated specials.
e: Eligibility is judged, not observed, and the frozen firm count is margin-bridged.
e: Some fitting businesses blend distribution, fabrication, installation, and manufacturing in ways public NAICS records do not reveal.
s5: No reliable six-digit transaction-rate or ownership-age measure was located.
s5: Consolidation headlines can overrepresent larger strategic deals and undercount small private transfers.
q: Commercial terms were not observed.
q: Retention likely differs sharply between commodity straight pipe and specialized fittings or certified custom systems.
d5: Infrastructure needs are not committed spending and include much more than pipe.
d5: BLS output is four-digit and broad.
d5: Material share between plastic, ductile iron, steel, concrete, and rehabilitation technologies can change.
o: No source directly measures the operator-required share.
o: Automation can shrink labor per unit without eliminating accountable manufacturing ownership.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business product sales under purchase orders, distributor programs, project bids, and supply agreements, generally priced per length, fitting, weight, or shipment with resin and freight adjustments.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive pipe-making units, installers and distributors without manufacturing, resin producers, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and manufacturers primarily classified in hose, profile, or other plastics codes.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying plastic pipe, conduit, and fittings to distributors, utilities, contractors, agricultural users, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations and wages for all NAICS 326100 plastics-product manufacturing, not pipe and fittings or task exposure. I map task fractions in planning, quoting, purchasing, administration, quality inspection, line monitoring, maintenance, packing, and material movement to current AI capability and weight toward the production-heavy payroll. Pipe extrusion is typically more continuous and already controlled than some molding segments, so broad-industry exposure likely overstates remaining opportunity; the estimate is reduced accordingly.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than 326122.
Task exposure is inferred rather than measured.
The estimate may miss differences between continuous pipe extrusion and more labor-intensive fitting fabrication or molding.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across PVC, HDPE, PEX, and fitting plants separating already-automated line control from manual inspection, lab testing, changeover, maintenance, material handling, planning, sales, and compliance work.
a.rationale: The BLS industry table shows production concentrated in metal/plastic workers, assemblers, other production workers, packing, and material movement, while management and business occupations are much smaller. AI can address vision inspection, predictive maintenance, documentation, forecasting, scheduling, and administrative work, but extrusion-line tending, setup, maintenance, handling, and shipping are largely physical and some controls are already automated.

d5.bridge: EPA measures twenty-year U.S. water-infrastructure investment needs, not five-year constant-quality demand for plastic pipe. The 2022 Clean Watersheds Needs Survey reports $630.1 billion of needs, including $151.1 billion for conveyance repair and new conveyance, and EPA's drinking-water assessment reports $625.0 billion of needs. Combined with BLS's 1.1% annual real-output projection for broad plastics-product manufacturing, these backlogs support modest five-year pipe demand growth. Funding constraints and competition from metal, concrete, and rehabilitation methods mean needs cannot be translated one-for-one into shipments, so the range includes flat demand.
caveats: Infrastructure needs are not committed spending and include much more than pipe.
BLS output is four-digit and broad.
Material share between plastic, ductile iron, steel, concrete, and rehabilitation technologies can change.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. shipment forecast by resin and end market using American Chemistry Council or Plastics Pipe Institute data, utility capital plans, Census construction-put-in-place data, and distributor sell-through volumes.
d5.rationale: Aging drinking-water, wastewater, stormwater, telecom, electrical, gas, irrigation, and construction networks create recurring replacement and expansion demand. EPA's documented infrastructure backlog is substantial, while BLS projects broad plastics-product output growth. Cyclicality, public funding delays, housing weakness, imports, alternative materials, and trenchless rehabilitation can offset the tailwind.

caveats: Eligibility is judged, not observed, and the frozen firm count is margin-bridged.
Some fitting businesses blend distribution, fabrication, installation, and manufacturing in ways public NAICS records do not reveal.
e.change_evidence: A census of the 59 frozen LMM-band candidates verifying primary manufacturing activity, independent ownership, external revenue, repeat-order share, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and transferable certifications and tooling.
e.rationale: Independent pipe and fitting manufacturers ordinarily provide repeat outsourced physical supply to external customers and fit the lens. Exclusions are captive operations, firms whose economics are actually distribution or installation, distressed plants without transferable relationships, and misclassified manufacturers dominated by adjacent products.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326122 ($2,365,875,749) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326122 ($18,062,479,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $713,115 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326122); class <5: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,421,574 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $208,261 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,864,806 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $273,194 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,059,059 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $887,652 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 59 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,369,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,105,066 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $102,759,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,054,252 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $358,852,541 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $52,571,897 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 59

caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share.
Automation can shrink labor per unit without eliminating accountable manufacturing ownership.
o.change_evidence: Utility, distributor, and contractor interviews on planned insourcing, imported sourcing, trenchless rehabilitation, material substitution, and acceptance of digitally produced or additively manufactured fittings.
o.rationale: Pipe and fittings are physical, standards-bound goods requiring an accountable manufacturer for extrusion or molding, material traceability, testing, certification, and delivery. Software cannot supply the current basket. The small residual risk is customer insourcing, imports displacing domestic output, or alternative non-pipe rehabilitation and material systems.

caveats: Commercial terms were not observed.
Retention likely differs sharply between commodity straight pipe and specialized fittings or certified custom systems.
q.change_evidence: Representative contracts and invoice histories measuring resin-index formulas, fixed-price duration, project rebid cadence, annual cost-down clauses, distributor rebates, customer concentration, and realized margin retention after documented process improvements.
q.rationale: An operator can retain savings during fixed-price quote periods, but many pipe products are transparent and competitively bid, while resin and freight adjustments make cost drivers visible. Distributors, utilities, and large contractors can demand annual concessions or rebid projects; certified products, service reliability, fabricated specials, and approved-vendor status create some switching friction.

caveats: No five-year implementation study specific to plastic pipe was found.
Adoption may be faster at standardized, high-volume plants and slower for short-run fittings or fabricated specials.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level evidence from 326122 operators showing AI use-case deployment rates, labor hours avoided, scrap and downtime changes, controls-integration costs, false-positive rates, and recertification requirements.
rho.rationale: Computer vision, predictive maintenance, demand forecasting, quote assistance, and document automation are deployable within five years, especially on repetitive lines. Implementation is constrained by legacy controls, fragmented sensor and quality data, small-firm engineering capacity, product-certification obligations, false-reject risk, cybersecurity, and the need for human release authority.

caveats: No reliable six-digit transaction-rate or ownership-age measure was located.
Consolidation headlines can overrepresent larger strategic deals and undercount small private transfers.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, CapIQ, Axial, and broker records of U.S. pipe and fitting manufacturer control transactions from 2016-2025 matched to an eligible-firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: Five-year control transfers should reflect ordinary private-company sale and succession activity, tempered by strategic ownership, family transfers, indefinite holds, minority recapitalizations, closures, and transactions in which a plant is absorbed without a transferable operating firm.

principal_driver: Large infrastructure replacement needs underpin recurring physical pipe demand while AI improves quality and uptime.
principal_weakness: Low labor intensity, existing line automation, and commodity price transparency limit the retained labor upside.

caveats: The labor-opportunity estimates extrapolate from four-digit plastics occupational data and generic DOE manufacturing use cases; neither source measures task exposure or deployability in NAICS 326122 plants.

The demand bridge combines twenty-year infrastructure needs with a ten-year four-digit output projection; it does not establish committed spending, plastic material share, or five-year pipe shipment growth.
The operator-required estimate is near one, but S3 and S4 document infrastructure needs rather than the share of current demand that must remain with an accountable plastic-pipe manufacturer.

The capped retention factor rests on unobserved commercial terms; the q range is honestly labeled ESTIMATE, but commodity versus specialized-product mix could materially alter realized retention without changing this record's robust STRUCTURAL_OUT tier.

Firm availability depends on a margin-bridged candidate count and judgmental eligibility and transfer rates without six-digit transaction or ownership evidence; this is disclosed and keeps the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

sources_audited.note: The BLS table directly confirms that it covers NAICS 326100, reports 605,750 workers, and shows the cited production, extrusion, molding, inspection, packing, management, and business occupation mix. It does not measure AI task exposure, which the packet correctly treats as a disclosed proxy transformation.

The DOE report directly describes automated visual quality control, advanced characterization, operations optimization, IoT-enabled predictive maintenance, and material sorting in manufacturing. It is not pipe-specific and does not establish five-year deployment rates, limitations the packet discloses.

The EPA page reports $630.1 billion of total clean-water needs, including $151.1 billion for conveyance repair and new systems and $115.3 billion for stormwater. The packet appropriately identifies the conversion from twenty-year needs to five-year pipe demand as a proxy rather than an observed relationship.

The EPA report confirms $625.0 billion of twenty-year state drinking-water need, including $273.1 billion for medium systems, $235.2 billion for large systems, and $100.1 billion for small systems. It does not directly measure plastic-pipe demand or operator-required share.

The BLS table reports NAICS 326100 real output of $190.7 billion in 2024 and $212.4 billion in 2034 with 1.1% annual growth, matching the packet. This remains a broad four-digit, ten-year projection rather than a six-digit five-year pipe forecast.

run_meta.title: Plastics Pipe and Pipe Fitting Manufacturing
